title: Triage every meaningful message into task updates
notes: Standing workflow rule now lives in Protocol instead of Workbench.
description:
Project memory

Goal
- Make sure meaningful messages get reflected in durable state instead of staying trapped in chat.

What changed
- This is no longer treated as an active Workbench project item.
- The behavior now belongs in the Protocol section as an ongoing operating rule.

Why
- It is a standing duty, not backlog work to be "completed" in the Workbench.

Where it lives now
- Mission Control > Protocol
- Protocol item: triage meaningful messages into task, event, or memory updates.

title: Optimize the mission-control website
notes: Mission control is LAN-accessible and now has a dedicated Protocol section so duties/recurring rhythm stop cluttering Workbench. Skills-page direction has now changed decisively: drop the flowchart idea and use concise summaries of how each skill works instead, because the chart effort burned too much time for too little payoff. The current UI task is to make that summary-style Skills view clear, elegant, and easy to scan while continuing the broader visual polish work.
description:
Project memory

Goal
- Keep improving mission control so it feels cleaner, more reliable, more LAN-friendly, and easier to use as the shared workspace.

What is known
- The current app now uses a stable single-server runtime on port 4180 instead of the earlier fragile split-origin setup.
- WSL-only binding was not enough for Wi-Fi/LAN access, so Windows needed a portproxy bridge and inbound firewall allowance.
- Haolun wanted the schedule and tasks to feel more clearly separated, with recurring duties shown as their own distinct box rather than blended into a generic side stack.

What has been done
- Refactored the frontend into cleaner view sections with shared mission-control utility helpers.
- Optimized calendar rendering by grouping scheduled tasks by day instead of re-filtering for every calendar cell.
- Reworked the schedule page so the calendar, upcoming scheduled tasks, recurring duties, and unscheduled work each have a clearer dedicated area.
- Cleaned up the toolbar/button styling and tightened the page structure.
- Switched the stable runtime to HOST=0.0.0.0 on port 4180.
- Updated the watchdog to monitor and restart the 4180 single-server runtime.
- Added a Windows portproxy from 0.0.0.0:4180 to the WSL address and added a private-profile firewall rule for TCP 4180.

Blockers / unknowns
- If the Windows LAN IP changes later, the portproxy target address may need to be refreshed if the WSL IP also changes.
- Real-world testing from another Wi-Fi device is still worth doing, even though Windows-local LAN-IP access now succeeds.

Next steps
- Test from another device on the same Wi-Fi using the Windows LAN IP URL.
- If the LAN IP changes later, refresh the reported URL and verify the bridge still works.
- Keep polishing based on real usage friction rather than abstract cleanup.

Visual polish feedback
- Latest Skills-page product correction from haolun: abandon the flowchart/graph idea entirely. Replace it with concise summaries of how each skill works, and judge success by readability/usefulness rather than diagram cleverness.
- Durable UI lesson: under-the-hood cleanup does not count as a successful visual fix unless the rendered result visibly resolves the complaint. For future UI/layout work, check the actual rendered outcome against the requested rules after each meaningful pass.
- Durable chart-routing rule from haolun: arrows should use orthogonal rounded routing with at most one total turn between points by default; avoid multi-elbow connectors unless there is a strong readability reason.
- Chart polish rule from haolun: streamline arrows/edges so they use the most direct readable route, with no unnecessary turns. Prefer clean side-anchored connections and aligned orthogonal branches over meandering manual paths.
- Chart-design rule from haolun: keep all text fully inside containers, avoid unnecessary line intersections/branch merging, keep branch lines aligned, and optimize first for clarity/legibility and then for elegance. Apply these rules to future skill charts by default.
- Skills-page follow-up: authored the next graph-worthy chart for workspace-memory-stack and changed the chart renderer to scale up to available panel width so large empty areas in the detail pane are used by the graph itself.
- Skills-page direction tightened: do not auto-chart every skill. Reserve diagram-style flowcharts for algorithmic skills where the reasoning process itself should be inspected (for example grounded-evolver and learning-loop), and use a cleaner reference-style presentation for the rest.
- Skills-page follow-up: the left-side skill cards were too cramped to read. Split the list into two sections (user-edited/created vs system) and give each skill card more breathing room before continuing polish.
- New product direction: add a Skills tab to mission control that shows installed skills and a readable flow chart of how hazoc reasons through each one. It does not need to be editable yet; the point is process visibility so haolun can refine the workflows.
- Strong correction on task controls: floating dropdowns keep fighting the card layout, clipping, and visual layering. Better design is to stop using popup menus here entirely and switch to inline segmented controls for lane/status changes.
- Native browser dropdowns looked out of place against the rest of the UI. Replace them with custom in-app menus for task section/status controls so the opened menu matches the site palette and feels intentional.
- Important correction from haolun: the archive problem was structural, not just visual. Archived rows were too squished and the status dropdown became unusably tiny. Treat Archived as a compact shelf/list mode with full-width rows and a real-width dropdown instead of reusing the active-task card layout unchanged.
- Sidebar polish direction: active navigation should feel visibly selected, including a richer gold dot / accent treatment on the active page item.
- General low-risk polish is welcome: smoother hover states, more intentional scroll areas, and small consistency upgrades that make the site feel finished rather than rough.
- Haolun also wants the archive selection state to feel smooth rather than choppy, and wants the task-section menu highlight to follow the same lane color scheme as the content below it.
- Haolun wants the selected task/detail state to be much clearer and smoother: stronger visual selection, bolder outline/accent, and generally nicer interaction when opening task details.
- Haolun explicitly does not want self-explanatory page-context boxes like “Why this exists” / “Keep workbench clean”; keep the UI focused on actionable state instead of explaining the product back to ourselves.
- Haolun said the current UI works on Mac now, but still looks a bit ugly.
- The detail panel/header treatment feels visually heavy and needs refinement.

Next polish targets
- Improve typography hierarchy and spacing.
- Reduce bulky dark blocks and harsh panel framing.
- Make the task detail view feel more elegant and integrated with the rest of the page.


Latest task-board refit
- Haolun said the Capture Tray had effectively gone unused and should be removed.
- Reworked the Tasks page into selectable subsections for Workbench, On Hold, and Archived.
- Swapped the old multi-column lane board for a denser single-section list plus detail panel so more tasks can stay visible at once while keeping long-form memory on the right.

Calendar polish update
- Haolun wanted the Schedule calendar to reflect scheduled events more clearly and to have more space.
- Enlarged the calendar area relative to the sidebar.
- Increased cell size and made scheduled days visually stronger.
- Event pills now show time + title so calendar entries read like actual scheduled items instead of tiny generic chips.

Event separation update
- Haolun wanted scheduled events separated from tasks.
- Added a dedicated events data store and API.
- Added an Events page where events can be reviewed, edited, and archived.
- Schedule calendar now reflects scheduled events, while scheduled work tasks remain separate in their own panel.
- Clicking a scheduled event now opens the Events page instead of the task board.


Latest planning
- While haolun is away from the desk, prefer low-risk internal optimizations instead of ambiguous product leaps.
- Best near-term queue: (1) tighten task/event/schedule UI polish into smaller concrete cleanup slices, (2) improve task-memory/context efficiency where noisy, (3) refine next-step implementation plans for market digest and multi-user scheduling so fresh sessions can pick them up cleanly.
- Added a dedicated Protocol section so standing rules and recurring duties (message triage, GitHub push rhythm, memory optimization, uptime, schedule routing, cross-channel mirroring) have their own dashboard home instead of bloating Workbench.

Away-time execution slices
- Visual hierarchy pass
  - tighten typography scale, heading hierarchy, and panel spacing
  - reduce heavy dark blocks / repeated framing
  - standardize chip/button/input emphasis across Tasks, Schedule, Events, and Memory
- Tasks + Events scanability pass
  - make row metadata easier to scan at a glance
  - ensure selected state and archived/on-hold status read clearly
  - trim noisy copy / redundant labels in detail panes
- Schedule / Events distinction pass
  - strengthen event-vs-task visual language
  - make live vs archived event state clearer
  - keep the schedule-intake panel visually integrated without competing with the calendar
- Low-risk QA / cleanup pass
  - click through Tasks, Schedule, Events, and Memory on the stable 4180 origin
  - verify persistence, empty states, and obvious copy mismatches
  - avoid touching watchdog / boot persistence unless a doc/state correction is clearly needed

Near-term order
- Start with visual hierarchy + scanability.
- Then do schedule/events distinction.
- Finish with a small QA sweep before considering more UI churn.

title: Track recurring duties on the schedule page
notes: Recurring duties were first made visible on Schedule; they now have a better permanent home in the Protocol section.
description:
Project memory

Goal
- Make recurring operational duties visible so hazoc does not neglect them.

What has changed since this was first built
- Recurring rhythm no longer needs to live on the Schedule page as its main home.
- A dedicated Protocol section now holds standing rules plus recurring duties so Workbench stays clean and Schedule can stay focused on dated items.

Outcome
- This earlier schedule-page milestone remains archived as part of the product history.

title: Push workspace changes to GitHub once per hour while active
notes: Recurring operational rhythm now lives in Protocol instead of Workbench. As of 2026-03-29, the push cadence is folded into the top-of-hour managed workspace-maintenance worker rather than a separate :30 cron.
description:
Project memory

Goal
- Keep meaningful workspace changes preserved on a steady cadence while active.

What changed
- This is no longer treated as a live Workbench item.
- The GitHub push rhythm now belongs in Protocol as recurring operational work.

Why
- It is a repeating duty, not a project backlog item.

Where it lives now
- Mission Control > Protocol
- Recurring rhythm: Push meaningful changes to GitHub.

title: Keep mission-control website up while active

title: Run hourly memory optimization + task board update while active
notes: Recurring memory-maintenance rhythm now lives in Protocol instead of Workbench. As of 2026-03-29, it is combined with git sync in the managed worker `maintain-workspace-hourly` so the memory pass runs first and git sync follows immediately after.
description:
Project memory

Goal
- Keep hourly memory optimization and task-board maintenance visible and durable.

What changed
- This is no longer treated as live Workbench backlog.
- The duty now belongs in Protocol as recurring operational rhythm.

Why

Where it lives now
- Mission Control > Protocol
- Recurring rhythm: Run hourly memory optimization + task board update.

title: Design market-moving news digest system
notes: Preferred first shippable shape: local-only, bounded, and auditable. Build order should be source allowlist -> observation schema -> scoring/dedupe -> affected-name mapping -> digest compiler, with external delivery deferred until digest quality feels stable. 2026-04-09 architecture recommendation: do not make a fresh one-shot web-scraper child the primary hourly loop. Prefer a deterministic low-thinking managed worker that wakes on cadence, fetches a fixed public-source allowlist, dedupes against local state, and only spawns a disposable low-thinking summarizer/verifier when there is enough new material to summarize. Hourly-from-scratch children are acceptable only as a rough prototype, not the efficient steady-state design. 2026-04-09: drafted worker-first v1 architecture in `skills/market-watch/references/hourly_digest_worker_v1.md` and temporarily parked the task until haolun returns to it after other agenda items.
description:
Project memory

Goal
- Build a daily / interval-based market-moving news digest system for haolun.

What is known
- Haolun does not want ticker-specific monitoring as the main model.
- The system should gather major public headlines that could significantly move markets.
- It should infer which sectors and important stocks are likely affected by each headline.
- It should later deliver digest summaries at chosen intervals.

Recommended source tiers
- Tier 1: official / primary sources (Fed, BLS, Treasury, SEC/EDGAR, company IR / press releases).
- Tier 2: major public financial/news sources (Reuters public pages, Yahoo Finance, CNBC public pages, MarketWatch, public RSS / Google News).
- Tier 3 later only if explicitly wanted: noisier public discussion sources.

Importance scoring rules to implement
- Macro impact (rates, inflation, jobs, war, regulation, energy, AI/chips, major earnings, M&A).
- Surprise / novelty.
- Breadth (broad market vs sector vs single-name).
- Repeated multi-source confirmation.
- Likely sensitivity of major stocks / ETFs / sectors.

Digest format
- Headline
- Why it matters
- Likely affected sectors / stocks / ETFs
- Estimated impact scope (broad / sector / single-name)
- Confidence / source quality

Delivery plan
- Interval-based digest rather than open-ended roaming.
- Good defaults to consider: morning setup digest, midday update, evening wrap-up, plus optional urgent alerts later.

What has been done
- The high-level product direction has been written into curated memory, USER.md, active-state, and today's daily note.
- A safer market skill base (`skills/market-watch`) already exists and can be extended.

Blockers / unknowns
- Exact final source allowlist still needs to be chosen.
- Need the desired delivery destinations and cadence confirmed.
- Need to decide whether the first version writes to files only, mission control only, or also pushes messages immediately.

Next steps
- Start with a bounded source list and digest scoring spec.
- Add public-source fetch/parse scripts under `skills/market-watch`.
- Store structured observations locally, then compile interval digests from that state.
- Later connect digest delivery to the chosen channels.

First shippable shape
- Local-only, bounded, and auditable.
- Compile morning / midday / evening digests from stored observations first.
- Leave urgent alerts for a later high-confidence threshold path.

Execution slices
- Source allowlist + cadence
  - finalize Tier 1 / Tier 2 pages or feeds
  - define per-source trust and freshness windows
- Observation schema
  - store id, timestamp, source, headline, summary, category, affected sectors / stocks / ETFs, score fields, confidence, and URLs
- Scoring + dedupe
  - rules-based scoring first
  - merge repeated coverage from credible sources
  - suppress stale repeats across digest windows
- Affected-name inference
  - start from a small curated sector / ETF / bellwether mapping
  - avoid an open-ended ticker universe in v1
- Digest compiler
  - rank accepted observations into morning / midday / evening bundles
  - keep outputs deterministic and link-backed
- Delivery boundary
  - start with local files or mission-control storage
  - add Discord / Telegram delivery only after digest quality is stable

Decision edges still to confirm
- Exact first delivery surface: files only, mission-control view, or both.
- Observation retention window and dedupe horizon.
- When a single-name story is important enough to include in a broader digest.

title: Design multi-user schedule intake and reminder architecture
notes: Current textbox stays as the intake layer. Cleaner build order: define profile registry + event ownership first, then clarification rules, then reminder routing, and leave Google Calendar linking for last.
description:
Project memory

Goal
- Prepare the next-stage architecture for multi-user scheduling in mission control without building it immediately.

What is known
- Haolun wants a Schedule-page text box that sends directly to hazoc for schedule requests.
- This should not be a generic manual event form; hazoc should interpret the request.
- The future schedule model should support 4 profiles: hazoc, haolun, and 2 additional users.
- A request may specify an event name, time, and optionally ask for the event to also be added to another user's schedule.
- Longer-term plan: link the user profiles to Google Calendar and let hazoc send reminders to each user through Discord and/or Telegram.

What has been done
- Captured the structure in durable memory and added this task to the board.
- Implemented the first piece: a direct Schedule-page textbox that sends requests to hazoc and shows inline replies / clarifying questions.

Blockers / unknowns
- The exact UX, profile model, Google-account mapping, and reminder-routing rules still need design.
- The broader multi-user/profile/integration work is still not to be implemented until requested.

Next steps
- Keep iterating on the direct schedule-intake panel as needed.
- Later design the per-user schedule model, cross-posting logic, calendar sync mapping, and reminder delivery rules.

Preferred build order
- Keep the current Schedule-page textbox as the intake layer.
- Define profile registry + event ownership next.
- Add clarification rules for ambiguous requests after that.
- Add reminder routing next.
- Leave Google Calendar linking / sync for last.

Execution slices
- Profile registry
  - fixed 4 profiles in v1: hazoc, haolun, user-3, user-4
  - per-profile label, default channel(s), active flag, and optional external calendar link placeholder
- Request interpretation contract
  - parse owner, title, start/end, optional second target, and optional reminder intent
  - clarify only when owner/date/time is ambiguous
- Event model
  - ownerProfileId
  - participantProfileIds or mirroredToProfileIds
  - reminderPolicy
  - source = hazoc-intake / manual / sync
  - keep syncState separate from core event data
- Reminder routing rules
  - per-profile preferred channel
  - reminders attach to events, not generic tasks
  - lead-time settings can remain simple at first
- Calendar sync boundary
  - external Google Calendar linking should be optional and last
  - keep internal mission-control events as the source of truth unless explicitly changed later

Open questions to preserve
- Does “add to another user's schedule” mean shared attendees or duplicated personal copies?
- Should profiles get separate timezones later, or stay New-York-first initially?
- Which profiles are allowed to receive automated reminders on which channel?

title: Keep custom market-watch and desktop-guard skills ready for later use
notes: Reviewed upstream ClawHub skills, rejected the risky/autonomous pieces, and built local hazoc-owned forks instead: `skills/market-watch` and `skills/desktop-guard`. Next likely extension for market-watch is the bounded market-moving news digest pipeline.
description:
Project memory

Goal
- Have local hazoc-owned versions of stock-analysis and desktop-control ready before haolun decides to use them.

What was reviewed
- `udiedrichsen/stock-analysis`
- `matagul/desktop-control`

Vetting summary
- `stock-analysis`: medium-high risk because the useful market tooling is mixed with Twitter/X token flows, rumor scanning, underspecified credential/storage behavior, and hidden home-directory persistence.
- `desktop-control`: medium risk because it is honest about scope, but screenshots/clipboard/input control are inherently sensitive and the autonomous AI agent layer increases risk.

What was built
- `skills/market-watch`: safer public-data market snapshots + local watchlist, no cookie scraping, no social auth, no hidden ~/.clawdbot state.
- `skills/desktop-guard`: guarded desktop primitives with dry-run-by-default mutating actions, no autonomous desktop agent layer.
- Packaged artifacts: `market-watch.skill` and `desktop-guard.skill`.

Next steps
- Leave these parked until haolun says to use or extend them.
- If needed later, flesh out features from the safer base instead of copying the upstream designs wholesale.

title: Configure Discord daily digests
notes: Future delivery target for broader digest systems, including the planned market-moving news digest pipeline.
description:
Project memory

Goal
- Configure Discord so hazoc can receive or synthesize daily digests that summarize what matters.

Why this exists
- Haolun wants the website calendar to eventually represent hazoc's own planned tasks, not raw Google Calendar events.
- One expected source for that future planning view is Discord daily digests.

What is known right now
- This work has not been configured yet.
- It is a dependency for the later planned-task schedule.
- It likely needs a clear digest format so hazoc can turn the digest into structured tasks or planning items.

What has been done
- Only the placeholder task and dependency tracking exist right now.
- The website already has a task board where this work is recorded.

Blockers / unknowns
- Exact Discord setup flow is still unknown.
- Need to decide whether the digest comes from a channel summary, bot message, cron job, or another workflow.

Next steps
- Configure the Discord side.
- Decide digest structure.
- Later connect that digest into hazoc's planning/calendar layer.

title: Configure Telegram daily digests
notes: Future delivery target for broader digest systems, including the planned market-moving news digest pipeline.
description:
Project memory

Goal
- Configure Telegram so hazoc can use daily digests as another input into planning and task formation.

Why this exists
- Haolun said the current website calendar is only a placeholder.
- The future version should show hazoc's planned tasks, and Telegram digests are one of the planned sources.

What is known right now
- This is not configured yet.
- It is parallel to the Discord digest work.
- The important outcome is not just getting Telegram connected, but turning digest content into useful planning memory.

What has been done
- The dependency is recorded in the mission-control task board.
- The placeholder calendar decision has been written into workspace memory.

Blockers / unknowns
- Need the exact Telegram digest workflow.
- Need to decide how hazoc should parse and store digest-derived tasks.

Next steps
- Configure Telegram digest flow.
- Define what counts as planning-worthy information.
- Connect it later to the planned-task schedule.

title: Build the mission-control task menu
notes: Task board exists and now uses three real sections: Workbench, On Hold, and Archived. The unused Capture Tray was removed.
description:
Project memory

Goal
- Build the website's task area into the shared memory for requests between haolun and hazoc.
- It should hold both active requests and postponed ones, so chat is not the only source of truth.

What has been decided
- The task board should be the place where new requests get recorded.
- The current task system uses three practical sections so it is easier to scan: Workbench, On Hold, and Archived.
- Each task should be clickable and should contain enough long-form project context that hazoc can relearn the project after losing short-term memory.

What has been done
- Built a local task board in mission-control.
- Added JSON-backed storage at mission-control/data/tasks.json.
- Added lane movement and seeded current project tasks.
- Restyled the site into a darker luxury look with gold accents.

What still matters
- Future new requests should be recorded here, not left only in chat.
- Over time, each task description should become a compact project journal: goal, decisions, work done, blockers, next steps.

Next steps
- Keep task descriptions current as real work happens.

What has been done
- Built the task board, detailed task view, luxury styling, and intake-by-conversation model.

title: Build a real schedule page for hazoc planned work
notes: Built the schedule page, then evolved it into an event-first calendar with tasks shown separately.
description:
Project memory

Goal
- Expand mission control with a real schedule page for scheduled events and future commitments, while keeping hazoc work tasks separate.

What is known
- Haolun wants the schedule grounded in mission-control state rather than Google Calendar as the primary source of truth.
- Times and dates should be correct.
- Future requests from haolun should be trackable there.

What has been done
- Added scheduling fields to tasks (`scheduledStart`, `scheduledEnd`).
- Added a real Schedule page with a monthly calendar and upcoming list.
- Evolved the schedule into an event-first calendar backed by a dedicated events store, with scheduled work tasks shown separately.
- Set the schedule display to America/New_York.
- Added scheduling inputs to task detail editing.
- Kept the old Google Calendar path as a placeholder API, separate from the main schedule page.

Blockers / unknowns
- Future scheduling still depends on hazoc actively assigning dates/times to the right container: Events for user-facing schedule items, Tasks for hazoc work.

Next steps
- As haolun asks for future scheduled items, record them in Events (or Tasks when the item is hazoc work) and keep the calendar aligned.

title: Optimize memory and task entries for context conservation
notes: Context-conservation rules are now written into AGENTS.md, USER.md, today's memory note, active-state, and MEMORY.md so fresh sessions can re-anchor from durable files instead of hauling old chat context. OpenClaw runtime compaction was tuned on 2026-03-29 so native auto-compaction now uses `mode: safeguard`, `reserveTokensFloor: 27200` (roughly a 90% threshold for the currently reported 272k session window), and a custom pre-compaction memory flush prompt that appends durable notes only to `memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md`. Restart-safe Discord closeout now also lives in durable state: qualifying direct-task completion pings are queued in `mission-control/data/main-task-closeouts.json` before dispatch so the handoff survives session or gateway interruptions better than a transient checklist. Managed workers now have a minimal workspace-local identity pattern under `workers/<worker-id>/`: read-only `spec.json`, writable `state.json`, and wake-time bootstrap through `scripts/bootstrap_worker.py`. The first dogfooded worker is `recover-main-task-closeouts`, and an end-to-end cron wake stamped `lastWakeSource=cron-recovery` successfully on 2026-03-29. On 2026-03-29 the hourly memory-maintenance duty was merged with GitHub sync into one managed worker, `maintain-workspace-hourly`, which runs at the top of the hour, performs memory/task upkeep first, then runs git auto sync immediately afterward if there are meaningful changes worth preserving. The old dedicated :30 git-sync cron was disabled. Standing-worker wakeups now replace throwaway per-task closeout sessions for Discord completion pings. The durable queue remains the source of truth, but immediate dispatch now happens by waking `recover-main-task-closeouts` instead of creating a one-shot cron helper each time. Session/runtime cleanup on 2026-03-29 pruned obsolete helper-session rows and removed the disabled legacy git-sync cron after the standing-worker architecture was validated. Backup snapshots were saved before deletion so the cleanup stayed reversible. Added lightweight agent namecards on 2026-03-29 for main, the Discord control-channel agent, and the guest-safe-web Discord sandbox under `agent-namecards/`. AGENTS now records the main-as-facility-manager rule plus the Discord branch hierarchy. Session labels were updated for the long-lived Discord identities. Direct session-to-session operating updates to those existing Discord sessions were attempted but blocked by OpenClaw session visibility limits from the current context, so the durable layer is in place even though immediate private transcript injection was not available here. Repo-organization cleanup on 2026-03-29 made the live-vs-archive boundary clearer: snapshots are now local-only/ignored, while `mission-control/data/README.md` and `memory/README.md` document what belongs in each live state area. Added a branch-importance rubric on 2026-03-29 under `agent-namecards/importance-rubric.md`. The Discord control and guest-safe-web namecards now define what counts as shared-important vs local-only memory, their state files now have pending-important arrays, and the hourly maintenance worker was upgraded to audit those arrays and promote unresolved shared-important items into main memory/task state when appropriate. Added lightweight agent bootstrap on 2026-03-29: `scripts/bootstrap_agent_identity.py`, `agent-namecards/index.json`, and main/control/guest-safe-web bootstrap commands inside the namecards. Also wrote `agent-namecards/promotion-pipeline.md` to capture the future system where agents default to their own local memory/state and cross-agent memory moves upward only through explicit promotion.
description:
Project memory

Goal
- Reduce context waste by making memory, task, and journal use more retrieval-driven.

What is known
- Haolun wants hazoc to search memory for relevant prior context whenever a new question arrives.
- Only the useful pieces should be loaded into working context.
- After answering, hazoc should ask whether that context will matter for the next conversation; if not, write/store what matters and avoid carrying the larger context forward.

What has been done
- Wrote the retrieval-loop rule into AGENTS.md and USER.md.
- Created MEMORY.md as a curated long-term anchor for mission-control direction, workflow rules, and current state.
- Updated this task so the behavior stays visible in mission control.

Blockers / unknowns
- Current-session history cannot literally be deleted from the model context mid-thread, but future retrieval can be kept much tighter.

Next steps
- Use memory search at the start of new meaningful questions.
- Keep retrieved context narrow.
- Store the durable result and avoid reloading unnecessary context later.

title: Build a Memory page for mission control
notes: Built a Memory page that reads the real workspace memory files and presents them like a journal with a detail view.
description:
Project memory

Goal
- Add a website memory section that works like hazoc's journal and helps preserve important context for future responses.

What is known
- Haolun wants a memory section similar in spirit to tasks, but functioning more like a journal.
- Important things should be written down via the existing workspace memory system so hazoc can remember them later and use them to improve responses.

What has been done
- Added an API endpoint that reads workspace memory sources directly.
- Added a Memory page with journal-style cards and a detail panel.
- Surfaced active state, curated context, and recent daily notes in one place.
- Kept it tied to the real workspace files instead of inventing a second memory database.

Blockers / unknowns

Next steps

- Larger abstract/cloud-heavy memory systems are not the right default for this workspace.

What has been done

- Adopted it as the default continuity approach in AGENTS.md, USER.md, and today's memory note.
- Added this task so the choice is visible in mission control too.

What has been done

Next steps

title: Combine reviewed self-improving skills into one grounded evolving algorithm
notes: Built a combined local evolution skill from the best safe parts of the reviewed memory/evolution skills.
description:
Project memory

Goal
- Combine the best safe parts of the reviewed self-improving skills into a single grounded evolving algorithm for hazoc.

What is known
- The useful pieces are proactive continuity, layered memory discipline, optional graph structure when relations matter, and a small human-supervised evolution loop.
- The unsafe pieces are autonomous daemons, external task hubs/markets, cloud memory sprawl, git self-repair/reset, and auto-publishing.

What has been done
- Read the custom replacement skills workspace-continuity, workspace-memory-stack, workspace-graph, and safe-evolution-loop together.
- Created a new combined skill named grounded-evolver.
- Added a planner script that scores signals and recommends the smallest safe mutation plus write destinations and validation checks.
- Tested the script and packaged the skill successfully to dist/grounded-evolver.skill.

Blockers / unknowns
- No blocker right now; the main thing is to use it as the default practical evolution frame.

Next steps
- Use grounded-evolver as the main synthesis layer when evolving behavior or preserving context.
- Keep the separate underlying skills as modular references when needed.

title: Review evolver skill and extract only safe evolution patterns
notes: Vetted suspicious evolver skill and rebuilt only the safe human-supervised evolution frame locally.
description:
Project memory

Goal
- Inspect the suspicious evolver skill carefully and rebuild only the safe, useful evolution ideas in a local skill.

What is known
- The registry marks autogame-17/evolver as suspicious, and the warning is justified.
- The safe core idea is a disciplined improvement loop: observe signals, choose a small change, validate, and record the result.
- The unsafe parts include autonomous loops, external hubs/tasks, git self-repair/reset logic, auto-publishing, and broad self-modification machinery.

What has been done
- Performed static vetting of package.json, index.js, key src files, ops modules, and risk-signaling code paths.
- Produced a short vetting report.
- Created a local replacement skill named safe-evolution-loop.
- Added a compact helper script for drafting safe evolution reports.

Blockers / unknowns
- No blocker; the key decision was to avoid copying the autonomous parts.

Next steps
- Use safe-evolution-loop when improving behavior from real failures or repeated friction.
- Keep improvement human-supervised and low-blast-radius.

title: Review elite-longterm-memory skill and make a custom version
notes: Inspected elite-longterm-memory and created a grounded local replacement skill.

Project memory

What is known

- The good underlying idea is a memory stack with active state, daily logs, and curated memory.

What has been done

- Vetted it as medium risk / suspicious-but-not-malicious.
- Created a custom local replacement skill named workspace-memory-stack.

Blockers / unknowns

Next steps

title: Review ontology skill and make a custom version
notes: Inspected oswalpalash/ontology and created a tighter local replacement skill.

Project memory

title: Create a custom workspace-continuity skill
notes: Inspired by ivangdavila/self-improving, but adapted to hazoc's actual workspace and task system.

Project memory

What is known

- It was also too sprawling for this workspace and wanted a parallel ~/self-improving tree that would compete with USER.md, MEMORY.md, daily notes, .learnings, and the mission-control task board.

What has been done

- Added concise references for routing decisions and task-memory structure.

Next steps

title: Replace placeholder calendar with hazoc's planned-task schedule
notes: Use Discord and Telegram daily digests as future input.
description:
Project memory

Goal
- Replace the current placeholder calendar in mission-control with hazoc's own planned-task schedule.

Why this changed
- The website briefly showed Google Calendar events through gog.
- Haolun clarified that this is not the real point of the calendar.
- The real purpose is to show hazoc's planned work and priorities.

What has been done

Current product direction
- The future schedule should be derived from hazoc's planning.
- Discord and Telegram daily digests are likely inputs.
- The website task system will probably feed into this too.

What remains

- Decide how to distinguish hard schedule vs soft intent.
- Build the schedule UI once the digest/task pipeline exists.

Next steps

- Then design the planned-task scheduling model.

title: Operationalize haolun's learning-system chart
notes: Canonical storage design is now in place: `.learnings/` is the draft/evidence area for lessons, while accepted protocols are indexed in `mission-control/data/protocol.json` and promoted into canonical operating files like `AGENTS.md`, `TOOLS.md`, or skill docs. Added a new global rule that when a request is decomposed into subtasks, protocols must be checked again for each subtask instead of assuming the parent scan covers the children. Tracked future follow-up: `task-protocol-registry-backfill`. Reset-anchor repair added on 2026-03-29: older handoff / "expected next move" notes are now treated as historical expectations unless corroborated by newer state; `memory/active-state.md` was rewritten to be live-only and AGENTS gained a freshness rule. Learning-run closeout is now being tightened into an explicit artifact pack: full detail in the day's `.learnings/.../error.md`, a succinct daily-note summary, and `.learnings` lesson/protocol entries, with accepted protocols still promoted into canonical files. Step-1 helper extraction is now done inside grounded-evolver: `scripts/prepare_outside_review.py` turns planner JSON into reusable preferred/fallback clean-room spawn payloads, and `scripts/snapshot_revert.py` provides cheap reversible whole-file snapshots under `.learnings/snapshots/`. Grounded-evolver docs/references were updated and both helpers were smoke-tested successfully on 2026-03-29. Update 2026-04-02: the learning/error layout cleanup is now implemented. Canonical readable error history lives in `.learnings/errors/YYYY-MM.md`, `.learnings/days/YYYY-MM-DD/error.md` is now archive-pointer/day-local context only, the legacy `.learnings/errors.md` landing page was retired into archive, the monthly ledgers were normalized, and the learning-loop scripts/docs/checkers were aligned to the new boundary.
description:
Project memory

Goal
- Turn haolun's learning-system chart into an actual operational workflow hazoc follows whenever there is a workflow/problem-learning case.

What is known
- Haolun wants a systematic fix, not ad hoc remembering.
- The likely job is to convert the chart into explicit triggers, decisions, actions, and logging/update steps across memory / tasks / .learnings / operating rules.
- Haolun first shared a Lucidchart edit/invitation link, then provided a readable screenshot of the chart itself.

What has been done
- Recorded the upcoming chart handoff in active-state and today's memory note so the reset does not lose it.
- Cleared old sessions so fresh chats can come up with the new default thinking/reasoning settings.
- Tested the original Lucidchart link from this environment; it reached Lucid but redirected to the register/login invitation gate instead of exposing the diagram contents.
- Received the chart screenshot and translated it into `skills/learning-loop/references/request-friction-protocol.md`.
- Updated `skills/learning-loop/SKILL.md` so the learning-loop skill explicitly points at the chart-derived request-friction workflow.
- Added a short post-request learning trigger rule to `AGENTS.md` so future sessions run the check consistently.
- Integrated the first request-friction trigger branch into `skills/grounded-evolver/scripts/grounded_evolve.py`.
- Added explicit detection for meaningful-request friction signals (user correction, tool failure, skipped step, protocol state, avoidable detour, bad local fix, repeat friction).
- Added branch outputs for `general-evolution`, `request-friction-first-time`, and `request-friction-protocol-failure`.
- Updated grounded-evolver docs (`SKILL.md` and `references/algorithm.md`) so the trigger branch is part of the skill definition, not just chat discussion.
- Tested both a normal progress case and a request-friction protocol-failure case through the planner successfully.
- Replaced the earlier three-way shape with an exhaustive two-branch model inside grounded-evolver: `build-pattern` vs `repair-pattern`.
- Removed `general-evolution` as a separate peer branch; signal kind is now tracked separately from branch choice.
- Rewrote `skills/grounded-evolver/scripts/grounded_evolve.py` so every signal now chooses exactly one branch and emits branch-specific packet fields, actions, validation checks, and next-step guidance.
- Updated grounded-evolver docs (`SKILL.md`, `references/algorithm.md`, and `references/mutation-matrix.md`) to reflect the new model.
- Reworked learning-loop handoff docs so grounded-evolver owns branch logic and learning-loop is the logging/promotion backend rather than a competing evolution system.
- Updated AGENTS so future sessions follow the new two-branch model instead of routing to the old adjacent request-friction protocol.
- Tested representative cases successfully: constructive progress -> `build-pattern`, first-time request friction -> `build-pattern`, repeated/protocol-backed request friction -> `repair-pattern`.
- Extended `skills/grounded-evolver/scripts/grounded_evolve.py` so it now emits branch reasons, structured problem packets, artifact checklists, self-authored prompts, and outside-review prompts instead of only classifying the branch.
- Added packet-filling inputs (`--context`, `--request-context`, `--symptom-type`, `--failure-mode`, `--outcome-type`, `--failed-layer`, `--protocol-name`) so real incidents can be captured cleanly instead of relying on vague defaults.
- Updated grounded-evolver docs so the planner is explicitly expected to generate operational artifacts, not just abstract guidance.
- Updated the learning-loop request-friction handoff so it consumes grounded-evolver packet/prompt output rather than redefining branch logic.
- Tested representative runs successfully after the change: benign progress capture, first-time request-friction build-pattern, and repeated/protocol-backed request-friction repair-pattern.
- Extended grounded-evolver again so outside review is now emitted as a literal clean-room subagent path: exact `outside_review_prompt`, exact `outside_review_spawn_plan`, and spawn isolation notes.
- Strengthened the outside-review prompt so the reviewer is instructed to ignore ambient context and treat the packet as the only allowed problem context.
- Added clean-room spawn guidance to grounded-evolver docs and the learning-loop handoff: use the prompt verbatim, do not prepend parent summary, prefer a sterile temp cwd outside the workspace, and keep the run one-shot with delete cleanup.
- Tested both build-pattern and repair-pattern request-friction cases to verify the planner now emits the exact prompt plus spawn spec in JSON output.
- Added rollback as a first-class planner output for protocol/rule/workflow mutations: `revert_required` plus a target-specific `revert_plan` with pre-change capture, rollback actions, and success condition.
- Added recommended actions and validation checks that require exact pre-change text capture and immediate rollback if vetting or validation fails.
- Updated prompts so both self-authored and outside-review plans call out reversibility when recommending protocol/rule/workflow changes.
- Updated grounded-evolver docs, learning-loop handoff guidance, and AGENTS so provisional protocol changes must remain reversible until accepted.
- Tested representative cases successfully: protocol-like repair-pattern signals now emit rollback requirements/plans, while benign progress signals do not.
- Defined the storage model for lessons vs accepted protocols: `.learnings/README.md` now marks `.learnings/` as the draft/evidence area, while accepted protocols must be promoted into canonical files and indexed in `mission-control/data/protocol.json`.
- Enriched the protocol registry with active status, scope, owner, and canonical-path fields so it can act as the visible index of accepted protocols instead of a loose summary list.
- Added a registry entry for the post-request grounded-evolver learning rule.
- Added a new global protocol for subtask protocol checks: when a request is decomposed into subtasks, check current protocols again for each subtask before acting.
- Updated `AGENTS.md` and learning-loop routing docs so the storage model and subtask rule are loaded operational behavior, not just task memory.

Blockers / unknowns
- The registry model now exists, but not every older AGENTS-level rule has been backfilled into `mission-control/data/protocol.json` yet.
- Accepted protocols still span multiple canonical files by design, so the registry must stay maintained as the index when new protocols are promoted.

Next steps
- Extracted step-1 reusable helpers into grounded-evolver: clean-room outside-review prep and snapshot/revert for safer reversible protocol edits.
- Gradually backfill remaining important AGENTS/skill protocols into the registry when they matter, instead of doing a giant one-shot dump.
- Keep `.learnings/` for draft/evidence only; promote accepted protocols into canonical files plus the registry.
- Use the new subtask protocol-check rule whenever future requests are decomposed into smaller actions.

title: Tighten git hygiene and continuous sync flow
notes: The one-time git-hygiene cleanup is done: Python cache artifacts are ignored/untracked, and the existing hourly auto-sync path remains the ongoing preservation rhythm. This no longer needs to sit in Workbench as active build work. Cleanup pass on 2026-03-29 removed tracked `.learnings/snapshots/*.json` backups from Git, added `.gitignore` coverage so future snapshots stay local-only, and added README maps for `mission-control/data/` and `memory/` to improve repo navigability without deeper structural churn.
description:
Project memory

Goal
- Keep the workspace repo continuously current without turning git history into noise.

What is known
- Haolun wants the entire git to stay constantly updated.
- The repo currently still tracks generated Python `__pycache__` / `.pyc` files, which adds avoidable churn.
- Durable state like mission-control data, memory files, and skill docs should remain trackable, while generated/runtime artifacts should not.

What has been done
- Identified current tracked noise sources, especially `skills/*/scripts/__pycache__/*.pyc`.
- Confirmed existing `.gitignore` already excludes obvious runtime directories like `tmp/`, mission-control build output, logs, and node_modules.
- Added Python cache ignores to `.gitignore` (`__pycache__/`, `*.pyc`).
- Untracked already-committed Python cache artifacts from the repo index.
- Reused the existing `scripts/git-auto-sync.sh` path instead of inventing a second git workflow.
- Ran the sync successfully and pushed commit `418f44b` to `origin/main`.
- Confirmed the hourly `sync-workspace-git-hourly` cron remains enabled at `:30` America/New_York.

Blockers / unknowns
- Continuous git hygiene still depends on keeping future generated/runtime artifacts out of the tracked set as new scripts/features appear.
- The current preservation rhythm is hourly, not per-minute; that matches the standing protocol and keeps history from getting noisy.

Next steps
- Keep using the existing hourly auto-sync path while active.
- Watch for other generated artifacts that should be ignored before they get committed.
- If haolun later wants more visibility, surface repo clean/dirty/ahead state in mission control rather than reopening this as generic backlog work.

title: Wire main task-completion pings into Discord
notes: Main-side completion pings now use the deliberately simple architecture: one lightweight best-effort isolated cron announce job to `hzwp / #main_updates` (`1487645227421536327`) with manual fallback info for `#control-center` (`1485016833819021495`). There is no standing recovery worker and task closure does not wait on delivery success. `scripts/main_task_closeout.py` writes a local ledger entry, builds the one-line Discord ping, and enqueues a one-shot send with `wakeMode=now`. On 2026-04-02, the two recent historical sends that looked suspicious in the ledger (`closeout-20260402-003` and `closeout-20260402-005`) were checked against cron run history and both were verified `delivered=true`; their ledger rows were reconciled accordingly. The helper was also tightened so new messages normalize punctuation/analogy phrasing (avoids `Like like ...`) and ledger temp writes no longer race on a shared `.tmp` filename during concurrent/manual reconciliation.
description:
Project memory

Goal
- Notify haolun in Discord whenever main fully finishes a directly assigned task, using a tiny analogy and a pointer back to the control UI, without spending disproportionate reliability overhead.

Current design
- Delivery route: `hzwp / #main_updates` (`1487645227421536327`).
- Fallback route: `hzwp / #control-center` (`1485016833819021495`) for manual fallback only.
- Runtime path: `scripts/main_task_closeout.py` records a local ledger entry and enqueues one isolated cron announce job with `wakeMode=now`.
- Reliability posture: best-effort only; missed pings are acceptable and do not block task closure.

Recent verification
- The 2026-04-02 closeout sends `closeout-20260402-003` and `closeout-20260402-005` were both verified delivered via cron run history.
- The helper now normalizes ping text and uses pid-scoped temp files so concurrent/manual ledger updates do not collide on a shared temp filename.

Next step
- Keep using the lightweight route by default. If haolun reports another missed ping, inspect actual cron run history / delivery behavior before reintroducing heavier recovery machinery.

title: Backfill older accepted protocols into the protocol registry
notes: Future cleanup task requested by haolun. The new registry/storage model is in place, but older important AGENTS/skill-level accepted protocols still need to be gradually indexed in `mission-control/data/protocol.json` so the registry becomes the complete active index instead of a partial one.
description:
Project memory

Goal
- Gradually backfill older accepted protocols into `mission-control/data/protocol.json` so active protocols have a visible registry/index in addition to their canonical operating files.

What is known
- The storage model is now split cleanly: `.learnings/` is the draft/evidence area, while accepted protocols should live in canonical files and be indexed in the protocol registry.
- The registry now supports `status`, `scope`, `owner`, and `canonicalPaths`.
- Not every older AGENTS-level or skill-level accepted protocol has been indexed yet.

What has been done
- Created the registry shape and backfilled the first important set of active protocols.
- Added the post-request grounded-evolver learning rule and the subtask protocol-check rule to the registry.

Blockers / unknowns
- Full one-shot backfill would be noisy and risks low-value dumping.
- Some older rules may need dedupe/merge decisions before indexing.

Next steps
- Backfill important older protocols gradually when they matter in active work.
- Skip low-value bulk dumping.
- Merge/normalize duplicates when they show up during backfill.
- Keep canonicalPaths accurate as protocols evolve.

title: Define hazoc identity and soul more explicitly
notes: SOUL.md and IDENTITY.md now have a conservative first pass, but identity refinement is intentionally slow. New standing rule: during nightly wrap-up, review today's memory for unusually defining moments and default to no change unless the signal feels strong and durable. Significant personality/identity edits still need to be surfaced to haolun first. Nightly identity curation ran on 2026-03-28 and produced no further edits beyond the first pass.
description:
Project memory

Goal
- Fill in SOUL.md and IDENTITY.md more deliberately so hazoc's presence is not left as a stub.

What is known
- Haolun previously wanted a later reminder to define hazoc's desired presence after setup.
- SOUL.md has not been revised since creation.
- IDENTITY.md is still mostly empty beyond the name hazoc.
- Haolun does not want overly significant changes made without being told first.

What has been done
- Confirmed both files are still near their initial state.
- Gathered the strongest current signals from behavior and existing docs: resourceful, grounded, proactive, concise, not performatively helpful, slightly sharp, warm when it matters.

Blockers / unknowns
- The exact creature/vibe/emoji/avatar choices still involve taste rather than purely operational facts.
- Bigger identity changes should be discussed before writing them.

Next steps
- First pass is now applied: SOUL has a modest companion/workshop-partner framing, and IDENTITY has provisional creature/vibe/emoji values.
- New refinement rule: during nightly wrap-up, review today's memory for unusually defining moments, but default to no change and keep edits extremely selective.
- Revisit later if hazoc's voice becomes clearer through more use or if haolun wants a bolder identity pass.
- Avatar is still intentionally unset.

title: Reduce background token burn while keeping main on xhigh
notes: New direction from haolun on 2026-04-02: keep main at `thinking=xhigh` and `reasoning=on`, but lower the cognitive budget for other agents/workers significantly to conserve weekly quota. Current diagnosis: the biggest drains were frequent wakes plus large startup prompts, especially the old 5-minute `recover-main-task-closeouts` worker and the hourly `maintain-workspace-hourly` worker. Persistent cost-control changes now applied: global default thinking is `low`, `main` stays `xhigh/on`, `main.subagents.thinking=low`, `guest-safe-web` defaults to `low/off`, and Discord `#control-center` is bound to a dedicated `discord-control` agent with `low/off`. The one-hour audit window from 10:47–11:52 EDT measured 228,539 total session-store tokens; `recover-main-task-closeouts` alone was about 181,074 (~79%), while `maintain-workspace-hourly` used about 47,461 (~21%). New preference from haolun: missed end-of-task pings are acceptable. On 2026-04-02 the closeout system was simplified accordingly: the standing recovery cron was removed, `scripts/main_task_closeout.py` now does a one-shot lightweight best-effort announce job plus local ledger entry, and task closure no longer waits on delivery success. Final optimization pass on 2026-04-02: compressed the always-loaded startup payload sharply (`AGENTS.md` 19.6k→6.9k chars, `USER.md` 5.1k→2.3k, `MEMORY.md` 8.1k→3.5k) by moving detailed direction/playbooks into recall-only reference files; disabled the default main heartbeat (`every: 0m`) while keeping future re-enable settings light/isolated; lowered default context ceiling to `160000`; increased compaction eagerness with `reserveTokensFloor=40000` and `softThresholdTokens=8000`; and patched `scripts/main_task_closeout.py` so closeout sends no longer depend on heartbeat (`--wake now`) and use `--at 5s` instead of the failing `+5s` syntax. Verified live status afterward: heartbeat is disabled for all three agents, default session window reports `gpt-5.4 (160k ctx)`, and the completion ping for this task was delivered successfully to Discord `#main_updates`.
description:
Project memory

Goal
- Keep the main direct session high-capability while making background/branch work much cheaper so weekly usage lasts.

What is known
- Main should stay `thinking=xhigh` and `reasoning=on`; background agents/workers should stay much cheaper.
- The biggest waste came from recurring wakes and oversized startup payload, not a hidden swarm of fresh reasoning turns.
- Old long-lived sessions may still show the previous wider token window until they compact or roll over, but new/default session setup now uses the lower 160k ceiling.

What has been done
- Global default thinking was lowered while keeping main on `xhigh/on`.
- Discord control routing was moved off main onto a dedicated cheaper agent.
- The heavy closeout recovery worker was removed and the closeout path simplified to one-shot best-effort delivery.
- The always-loaded startup files were compressed hard and detailed product/chat guidance moved into recall-only references.
- Default main heartbeat was disabled, compaction was made earlier/aggressively memory-safe, and the closeout helper was patched to wake immediately without heartbeat dependency.

Next steps
- Discuss the longer-term cheap memory / recall-first design for main and whether future subagents should get an even smaller retrieval-oriented startup path.

title: Design a cheaper recall-first memory path for main/subagents
notes: Follow-up requested by haolun after the token-efficiency pass. The cheap-recall path now has a real retrieval boundary and summary layer: startup uses the small operating spine; compact task/daily anchors are auto-built under `tmp/recall-anchors/`; `scripts/recall_index.py` and `mission-control/data/recall-sources.json` now prefer those anchors before raw task prose or raw daily logs; and the regression harness covers re-anchor/history/preference/protocol plus task-anchor and daily-anchor behavior. Open next decisions: whether mission-control itself should consume compact task summaries directly, and whether the large frontend (`mission-control/src/App.jsx`) should be split in the next cleanup pass. New priority from haolun on 2026-04-09: critique and slim the always-loaded prompt surface itself, especially duplicated rules across auto-loaded root files and any unnecessary context that main/subagents keep re-reading every request. Likely next mutations: shrink AGENTS/TOOLS/USER/MEMORY overlap, keep `memory/active-state.md` strictly live and tiny, and enforce packet-only/one-shot defaults for helpers unless persistence is explicitly worth the token cost. Implemented first disposable-helper pass on 2026-04-09: added `subagent-classes/` with starter one-shot class specs, made `scripts/build_subagent_brief.py` class-aware via `--class-id`, updated AGENTS so disposable helpers default to class-compiled packets while persistent actors keep deterministic ids, and validated one real narrow subagent run. Current caveat: spawn attachments are disabled in this runtime, so end-to-end tests currently inline excerpts rather than attaching them. 2026-04-09 root/startup slimming pass completed with rollback + tests: added `mission-control/data/prompt-surface-spec.json` and `scripts/validate_prompt_surface.py`, shrank the root/startup files to boot-ROM style, and validated rule coverage plus budgets successfully. Char totals moved from root/startup/combined `20100/10419/30519` to `5037/4608/9645`.
description:
Project memory

Goal
- Design the next-stage cheap memory / recall approach after the immediate token-governance fixes.

What is known
- Hard-file memory plus targeted recall is the desired direction.
- Built-in OpenClaw context pruning is not the main lever for the current `gpt-5.4` main session.
- The immediate high-value wins from recurring wakes and oversized startup payload are already applied.

Start here next session
- Re-anchor from `memory/active-state.md`.
- Use this task as the working brief.
- Keep the output short and architectural rather than immediately rewriting more files.

Questions to answer
- What should always load for main versus remain recall-only?
- What is the smallest reliable startup bundle for future subagents/helpers?
- How should recall/search work so memory stays cheap in tokens without becoming fragile?
- Which gains come from startup trimming versus routing/workflow changes?

Next steps
- Define the exact always-load bundle for main (identity core, active-state, tiny current-focus anchor) and keep everything else explicitly recall-only unless needed.
- Write a tiny recall map / startup contract so future sessions know where to look for preferences, task state, recent timeline, and detailed protocols without loading them all.
- Codify a request triage routine: answer directly if no prior context is needed; otherwise retrieve narrowly from curated memory, task board, daily notes, or protocol refs in that order.
- Define an extra-cheap subagent brief format: task goal, constraints, expected output, and only the minimal excerpts actually required.
- Validate by testing fresh-session re-anchoring and a few representative task/subagent cases, then compare token/status behavior before deciding whether more file surgery is needed.
- Revised constraints from haolun: token efficiency must not compromise intelligence; the full recall map should be written out for review; the search system should be able to sift the whole memory stack when needed while still minimizing tokens; and task-local subagent context should be baked into code rather than left as a soft convention.

Implemented artifacts
- `PROTOCOL_SPINE.md` as the always-loaded high-importance rule layer.
- `RECALL_MAP.md` as the explicit routing map for context lookup.
- `scripts/recall_index.py` to build/search a derived local recall index across curated memory, daily notes, tasks, protocols, references, namecards, and worker state.
- `scripts/build_subagent_brief.py` to generate minimal-by-default helper packets with explicit `minimal` / `targeted` / `extended` context modes.
- `AGENTS.md`, `USER.md`, `MEMORY.md`, and the protocol registry updated to make the behavior durable.

Validation now needed
- Fresh-session re-anchor test
- Project resume test
- Preference/history recall test
- Protocol-heavy recall test
- Narrow helper/subagent packet test

Optimization batch (2026-04-02 evening)
- Added `scripts/recall_anchor_lib.py` and `scripts/build_recall_anchors.py` to derive compact task/daily anchors.
- Updated `scripts/recall_index.py` to auto-build and index those anchors, with scoring/routing that prefers them before raw prose-heavy sources.
- Updated `mission-control/data/recall-sources.json`, `PROTOCOL_SPINE.md`, `RECALL_MAP.md`, `AGENTS.md`, and `protocol.json` to encode the retrieval boundary rule.
- Extended `mission-control/data/recall-regression.json` so anchor-first behavior is tested, not just implied.

title: Investigate webchat unresponsiveness and disappearing messages
notes: Triggered by the 2026-04-02 main control-ui incident where hazoc stopped responding for a while and new webchat messages appeared to disappear until haolun restarted the session. The prerequisite learning-storage cleanup and closeout-path repair are now complete, so this follow-up is ready for active investigation.
description:
Project memory

Goal
- Find the smallest durable fix for the main webchat/control-ui failure where hazoc became unresponsive and incoming messages appeared to disappear.

What is known
- The incident happened during the 2026-04-02 evening session while work was in progress on learning/error-layout cleanup and the Discord closeout path.
- Haolun had to restart hazoc to recover.
- The problem is already logged in `.learnings/errors/2026-04.md` as `ERR-20260402-001` and in `memory/2026-04-02.md`.

Next step
- After the current requested implementation is complete, inspect the restart window, session/transcript state, and webchat delivery behavior to isolate whether the failure lived in the UI, transcript/session store, or the agent runtime itself.
